planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; satin grass grows naturally in coastal scrub, dry sandy pinelands, and coastal strand of south Florida and the Gulf Coast. Division of established clumps in spring is most reliable for propagation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; satin grass (formerly Muhlenbergia filipes, now M. sericea) is a coastal grass of south Florida, the Gulf Coast scrub, and coastal hammocks, producing extraordinary rosy-purple to pinkish plumes in fall that create a shimmering cloud of color above the fine-textured foliage. More cold-tender than the common pink muhly grass (M. capillaris) but similar in appearance, with more delicate, silkier, purple-pink plumes. Native to coastal scrub and dry pinelands of Florida and the Gulf Coast, it thrives in sandy, low-nutrient, well-drained soils where it is drought-tolerant once established. The fall plume display in September–November rivals any ornamental grass for pure visual impact. A superb native for coastal gardens and dry, sandy landscapes in zones 8b–11.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline soil in full sun; deer grass grows naturally in dry washes, chaparral margins, oak woodland edges, and desert grasslands of the southwestern US and Baja California.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; deer grass is a large, dense, mounding native grass of the southwestern US, from California to New Mexico and Texas, growing naturally in dry washes, canyon slopes, and the margins of chaparral and oak woodland. The plant forms an attractive, tight dome of fine-textured, grey-green to blue-green foliage that remains evergreen in mild climates. In summer and fall, long, straight, narrow flower spikes rise well above the foliage like thin wands, creating an architectural effect quite different from the plumed muhly grasses. Extremely drought and heat tolerant — one of the toughest ornamental grasses for hot, dry, western gardens. Deer resistant (as the name implies) and fire-adapted. Widely used in California, Arizona, and Texas in low-water, heat-tolerant landscaping. Division of mature clumps every 3–4 years maintains vigor.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, rocky, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas muhly grows naturally on dry, rocky, limestone slopes and canyon walls of the Edwards Plateau and Trans-Pecos of Texas. It is adapted to very dry, calcareous, vertical or steep rocky substrates.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas muhly is a small, tufted native grass of the limestone hills and rocky canyon walls of central and west Texas, adapted to the thin, calcareous, excessively well-drained soils of the Edwards Plateau. The delicate, airy seed heads in late summer and fall create a fine-textured, golden haze above the compact green clumps. Unlike the larger, showier muhlys (M. capillaris, M. sericea), Texas muhly is a fine-textured, more compact plant appropriate for smaller-scale rocky, dry garden settings and rock garden pockets in calcareous soil. Excellent for crevices in limestone walls and dry rock garden beds in zone 9a. Plants grown in rich, moist soil tend to flop; the lean, dry conditions of its native limestone habitat produce the best compact form.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, clay-loam to rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple needlegrass is the state grass of California, native to clay grasslands, serpentine outcrops, and coastal prairie. In its native climate it is winter-growing, summer-dormant, and best sown in fall for natural germination.

directions: Plant in well-drained, clay-loam, rocky, or sandy soil in full sun; purple needlegrass is the state grass of California and one of the most important grasses of native California coastal prairie and valley grassland. The fine, dark green foliage forms elegant, upright bunches that are evergreen in mild coastal climates. In spring, the plant produces distinctive, feathery purple-tinted seed heads with long, twisted awns that catch the light and shimmer purple in morning and evening sun — giving the plant its common name. After the summer-dry seed-set period, the awns help the seeds burrow into the soil by hygroscopic twisting, planting themselves. An outstanding low-water, deer-resistant native grass for California-style gardens and drought-tolerant plantings. Winter-active, summer-dry cycle; do not irrigate in summer once established in California climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; roundleaf alumroot grows naturally on rocky slopes, cliff faces, and talus in the western mountains and is unusually drought-tolerant for a heuchera. The cultivar 'Green Ivory' is widely grown.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; roundleaf alumroot is a western North American native of rocky slopes, cliff faces, and alpine talus from British Columbia to California and east to the Rockies, adapted to excellent drainage and some drought. The foliage forms low mounds of rounded, dark green, gently lobed leaves that are semi-evergreen. The flower spikes in late spring are distinctively cylindrical (reflecting the species name) with small, creamy-yellow to greenish-white flowers packed tightly along the spike. More drought-tolerant than eastern heuchera species, making it well-adapted to western gardens with dry summers. The cultivar 'Green Ivory' is widely used in rock gardens and xeriscape plantings. An important parent of some modern heuchera hybrids. Longer-lived and more drought-tolerant than most hybrid heucheras.
